chunks.text: \chapter{Network News}\index{network news} {\it See the file /usr/pub/news.help for the most current information. } \section{What is Network News?} The EECS Instructional Group supports a form of electronic discussion group called {\it network n

electronic discussion group called {\it network news}. It is sometimes also called {\em Usenet} or simply {\em newsgroups}.\index{newsgroups} Discussions covering hundreds of topics are occurring on network news. Some of the discussions are academ

network news. Some of the discussions are academic or focused on a specific discipline. Others cover a wide range of miscellaneous topics. You can find discussions about public domain software on Macintosh computers as well as discussions of poli

Macintosh computers as well as discussions of politics in China. You'll find recreational topics like bicycling and radio-controlled aircraft. In short, nearly any popular topic is being discussed in a newsgroup. At Berkeley, we also have a special

a newsgroup. At Berkeley, we also have a special newsgroup for each EECS course, such as {\tt ucb.class.cs61a} or {\tt ucb.class.ee105}. \section{Campus Newsgroup Server} We subscribe to most newsgroups and place the discussions from the last few

groups and place the discussions from the last few weeks on a newsgroup server. You can subscribe to newsgroups if you have access to a campus UNIX machine. The discussion in this chapter focuses on the UNIX\TM\ command {\bf trn}\index{trn@{\bf trn}

the UNIX\TM\ command {\bf trn}\index{trn@{\bf trn} command} which is available on Instructional workstations. There is also another news reader called {\bf nn}. It is similar to {\bf trn}, but has more (and better) features. Type ``man nn'' or read

re (and better) features. Type ``man nn'' or read the file /usr/pub/news.help on any Instructional machine for details. \section{Using {\bf trn} the First Time} \index{trn@{\bf trn} command!first use} The {\bf trn} command (short for {\bf t}hreade

e} The {\bf trn} command (short for {\bf t}hreaded {\bf r}ead {\bf n}ews) is used to access news in your favorite newsgroups. By default, you are subscribed to all newsgroups. Your first {\bf trn} session is usually spent unsubscribing from newsgr

session is usually spent unsubscribing from newsgroups that you don't want. \index{newsgroups!unsubscribing from} When you enter the {\bf trn} command, you are presented with a short message from the newsgroup administrator and prompted for each new

newsgroup administrator and prompted for each newsgroup. You receive a message of the form: \begin{quote} {\tt ******** 21 unread articles in news.announce.newusers--read now? [ynq] } \end{quote} You will receive a message like this for every news

ou will receive a message like this for every newsgroup on the news server. Since there are a few hundred newsgroups, you will want to unsubscribe from unwanted newsgroups in the most efficient manner. Here are few tips:\index{trn@{\bf trn} command!s

. Here are few tips:\index{trn@{\bf trn} command!subcommands} \begin{itemize} \item Enter {\bf u} to {\bf u}nsubscribe from a group. This will be your most common response during your first session. It is how you will discard newsgroups you don't w

It is how you will discard newsgroups you don't want. If you accidentally unsubscribe from a newsgroup that you want, don't worry; it is easy to re-subscribe. \item Enter {\bf n} to signify that you don't want to read this newsgroup now. Enter th

u don't want to read this newsgroup now. Enter this during your first session when you want to remain subscribed to a group. You can read the newsgroup later. Remember, if you don't like what you read you can always unsubscribe. \item Enter {\bf y

ad you can always unsubscribe. \item Enter {\bf y} if you want to start reading this newsgroup. \item Enter {\bf q} to quit {\bf trn} for now. \item Be sure to subscribe to the newsgroup named {\tt news.announce.newusers}. It contains several pos

news.announce.newusers}. It contains several postings that describe newsgroup etiquette, style and manners. Many newsgroup administrators consider this information to be required reading for their newsgroup readers. \item A tip for experts: If y

newsgroup readers. \item A tip for experts: If you feel comfortable with {\bf vi} or {\bf emacs} you might want to enter {\bf q} at the first prompt and edit the file named {\tt .newsrc}. This file contains a list of all newsgroups, a subscription

contains a list of all newsgroups, a subscription indicator, and a list of the last article you have read in a particular newsgroup. If a newsgroup name is followed by a colon ``:'' you are subscribed to it. If a the name is followed by an exclama

ed to it. If a the name is followed by an exclamation point ``!'', you are unsubscribed from it. You can globally change the ``:'' to ``!'' and unsubscribe from all newsgroups. Then you can manually subscribe to desired groups by replacing the ``!

y subscribe to desired groups by replacing the ``!'' with a ``:''. This method of selecting newsgroups is a shortcut that eliminates a few hundred prompts (and responses to prompts). \end{itemize} \section{Reading News with {\bf trn}} \subsection

\section{Reading News with {\bf trn}} \subsection{Notification of New Newsgroups} When you decide which newsgroups to read, you can begin using {\bf trn} to read newsgroups. When you enter: \begin{quote} {\tt \% }{\bf trn}\index{trn@{\bf trn} comm

quote} {\tt \% }{\bf trn}\index{trn@{\bf trn} command} \end{quote} you are presented with a list of newsgroups that are new and have become available since the last time you read news. You are asked if you want to subscribe and you can reply {\bf y}

if you want to subscribe and you can reply {\bf y} or {\bf n}. Usually only one or two groups are presented, so this is not too time-consuming. \subsection{Summary of Newsgroups with New Articles} Information about which articles you have read are

nformation about which articles you have read are stored in the file named {\bf .newsrc}. This information is used to determine which articles you have read and which are new. When you invoke {\bf trn}, it checks to see if there are new articles si

rn}, it checks to see if there are new articles since the last time you read news. If so, you are notified about the groups that have new articles and how many you have not read. The calculation of how many articles you have not read is approximate

how many articles you have not read is approximate. The sequence number of the last article you read is compared to the sequence numbers of articles on the news server and a calculation occurs to create the count. If you use {\bf trn} regularly, th

ate the count. If you use {\bf trn} regularly, the count of new articles should be fairly accurate. However, the very first time you read a newsgroup, you can expect the count to be high. This is because articles age from our news server after seve

cause articles age from our news server after several weeks. There may be gaps in the sequence numbers which distort the count. \subsection{Newsgroup Selection}\index{newsgroups!selecting} After you have been notified of any new newsgroups, you ar

u have been notified of any new newsgroups, you are placed in the newsgroup selection mode of {\bf trn}. If there are new articles in any of your newsgroups, you receive a message of the form: \begin{quote} {\tt ******** 21 unread articles in news.

{quote} {\tt ******** 21 unread articles in news.announce.newusers--read now? [ynq] } \end{quote} At this point, you have several choices. Some of the most common commands are:\index{trn@{\bf trn} command!subcommands} \begin{description} \item [y]

ommand!subcommands} \begin{description} \item [y] or press the \key{spacebar} to select this newsgroup and start reading the articles in it. \item [n] to skip to the next newsgroup with unread articles. \item [q] to quit {\bf trn}. \item [=] selects

s. \item [q] to quit {\bf trn}. \item [=] selects this newsgroup and displays subjects of the articles in it. \item [c] to mark all the articles in this newsgroup as being read. You might want to do this the first time you encounter a newsgroup with

his the first time you encounter a newsgroup with a few hundred articles that you haven't read or if you have been on vacation for awhile and don't want to read old articles. \item [u] to unsubscribe from this newsgroup. \item [g] to specify (``go''

from this newsgroup. \item [g] to specify (``go'' to) another newsgroup. If you aren't currently subscribed, you are asked if you want to subscribe. This is a way to subscribe to a newsgroup you don't currently read. For example, suppose you want

't currently read. For example, suppose you want to subscribe to a group named {\tt soc.culture.china}, enter the command: {\bf g soc.culture.china}. \item [h] to see a list of commands that you can enter in the newsgroup selection mode. \end{descr

enter in the newsgroup selection mode. \end{description} The newsgroups are presented in the order they are listed in your {\tt .newsrc} file. If you want to display newsgroups in a different order, edit the {\tt .newsrc} file and rearrange the ne

, edit the {\tt .newsrc} file and rearrange the newsgroups in a suitable manner. \subsection{Article Selection}\index{newsgroups!articles!selecting} Once you have selected a newsgroup you are in the article selection mode\index{trn@{\bf trn} comman

article selection mode\index{trn@{\bf trn} command!article selection mode} of {\bf trn}. Articles that are new since the last time you used {\bf trn} can be selected. If you selected a newsgroup by pressing the \key{spacebar} or by entering {\bf y}

pressing the \key{spacebar} or by entering {\bf y} you automatically entered article selection mode and selected the first article using the lowest numbered article as the first article. You may see one of two prompts. \begin{itemize} \item If the a

one of two prompts. \begin{itemize} \item If the article was too long to display on the screen, you see a prompt that is much like the prompt produced by {\bf more}. This is the prompt of the {\bf trn} pager mode which works very much like the {\bf

n} pager mode which works very much like the {\bf more} command. You can press the \key{spacebar} to see more of the article. However, unlike {\bf more} you can enter article selection commands also. \item If the article was short enough to display

. \item If the article was short enough to display on one screen, you see the article selection prompt which looks similar to the following prompt: \begin{quote} {\tt End of article 10 (of 19)--what next? [npq]} \end{quote} \end{itemize} If you ente

ext? [npq]} \end{quote} \end{itemize} If you entered ``{\bf =}'' to see a summary listing of the articles in a newsgroup, your session is placed at the article selection prompt when the end of the summary is reached. Article selection mode has feat

mmary is reached. Article selection mode has features that enable you to: \begin{itemize} \item Select articles based on the subject and article number. \item Unselect articles if you decide that you want to avoid a particular subject or author. \it

want to avoid a particular subject or author. \item Post articles of your own. \item Respond to articles. \end{itemize} \subsection{Default Selection} If you pressed the \key{spacebar} to select a newsgroup, the first article of the newsgroup is a

newsgroup, the first article of the newsgroup is automatically displayed. If all that you want to do is page sequentially through the articles, keep pressing the \key{spacebar}. The \key{spacebar} is a synonym for the {\bf n} command; both commands

s a synonym for the {\bf n} command; both commands display the next article in the sequence of articles. Since the \key{spacebar} is also how you page through long articles, you can page through all the articles in a newsgroup by pressing the \key{s

the articles in a newsgroup by pressing the \key{spacebar}. \subsection{Selection Commands} \index{newsgroups!articles!selection commands} \index{trn@{\bf trn} command!article selection mode!commands} There are numerous commands for article selecti

} There are numerous commands for article selection. Some of the popular commands are listed in this section. For a more complete discussion, enter the command: \begin{quote} {\tt \% }{\bf man trn} \end{quote} and read the discussion of article se

\end{quote} and read the discussion of article selection. Some of the most popular commands are: \begin{description} \item [h] to see a list of commands that you can enter in the article selection mode. \item[=] displays the subject line and sequenc

de. \item[=] displays the subject line and sequence number of every unread article. This is a good way to see an ``index'' of the unread articles. \item[{\em sequence\_number}] displays the article whose sequence number you have entered. \item[n] or

hose sequence number you have entered. \item[n] or {\bf N} or \key{spacebar} scans forward and displays the next article. \item[p] or {\bf P} displays the previous unread article, if any article is available. \item[\ctrl{n}] scans forward and display

ailable. \item[\ctrl{n}] scans forward and displays the next article with the same subject. \item[\ctrl{p}] scans backward and displays any previous article with the same subject. \item[m]marks the article as being ``unread''. You might do this if y

ticle as being ``unread''. You might do this if you want to come back to this article later. \item[M]marks an article as unread but only after you have finished all the articles in this newsgroup. You use {\bf M} rather than {\bf m} if you don't wa

ou use {\bf M} rather than {\bf m} if you don't want a marked article to be displayed again during this {\bf trn} session but want to read it in subsequent {\bf trn} sessions. \item[j] ``junks'' the current article by marking it as having been read a

urrent article by marking it as having been read and displays the prompt you would normally receive after reading the article. \item[k] ``kills'' every article with the same subject as the current article by marking it as having been read. \item[\^{

icle by marking it as having been read. \item[\^{ }] displays the first unread article. \item[\$] goes to the end of this newsgroup. This also has the effect of positioning your session ``past'' the last article, where there is no current article.

last article, where there is no current article. You issue this command prior to posting a new article to the newsgroup. If you don't, the article you post is associated with the article you are reading. \end{description} \subsection{Posting Articl

ing. \end{description} \subsection{Posting Articles}\index{newsgroups!articles!posting} \index{trn@{\bf trn} command!article selection mode!commands} You can post articles of your own, either in response to an article or as a new article of your ow

ponse to an article or as a new article of your own. The news articles you post may be read by anyone. People who post are expected to follow accepted rules of behavior - no obscenities, no offensive remarks of a racial or sexual nature. The Univer

e remarks of a racial or sexual nature. The University is bound by the laws and policies that protect its members from insult and harassment and can not permit University facilities (i.e. the local news servers) to be used for such purposes. There

ews servers) to be used for such purposes. There are two ways to post articles of your own. The easiest way is by running the {\bf Pnews}\index{Pnews} program. Just type {\tt Pnews} at the shell prompt, answer the questions, and then enter your art

mpt, answer the questions, and then enter your article. Type ``man Pnews'' for more information. The second way to post an article is by entering one of the following commands at the article selection prompt inside {\bf trn}: \begin{description} \i

on prompt inside {\bf trn}: \begin{description} \item[f] submits a followup article. If you are positioned on a non-existent article such as ``End of newsgroup'' (which you can get to with the \$ command), then the article is treated as an original

mand), then the article is treated as an original article. Otherwise, the article is treated as a followup to the current article. \item[F] submits a followup article and includes the old article with lines prefixed by $>$; {\bf trn} tries to provid

lines prefixed by $>$; {\bf trn} tries to provide an attribution line in front of the quoted article based on the contents of the {\bf From:} line. \end{description} Be sure you have read the articles in the newsgroup {\tt news.announce.newusers}

les in the newsgroup {\tt news.announce.newusers} before you post an article. This newsgroup contains articles written by newsgroup administrators that explain newsgroup etiquette, style and conventions. The articles contain valuable information fo

ons. The articles contain valuable information for new newsgroup users. Be especially careful when you post an article. Think through what you say before you actually post to your newsgroup. What you post will go to hundreds or perhaps thousands o

ou post will go to hundreds or perhaps thousands of recipients. A posting is all these people know about you; it should accurately represent your views and demeanor. Additionally, be considerate with your use of network resources. Don't waste bandwi

your use of network resources. Don't waste bandwidth. \subsection{Replying to Articles Through Mail} \index{newsgroups!replying by mail} \index{trn@{\bf trn} command!article selection mode!commands} As you are reading an article you may find that

As you are reading an article you may find that you want to reply to the author but not post a reply to the entire newsgroup. Perhaps you want to send a personal comment to the author. Rather than post an article, use one of the following reply c

post an article, use one of the following reply commands: \begin{description} \item[r] replies to an article through network mail. \item[R] replies to an article through network mail and includes the old article with lines prefixed by $>$. \end{desc

old article with lines prefixed by $>$. \end{description} Reply directly to the author of an article whenever your comments are personal or fall outside the scope of the discussion. It is inappropriate to post personal comments to the entire newsgro

te to post personal comments to the entire newsgroup. Also, postings which waste network resources are usually not appreciated. \subsection{Saving an Article as a File} \index{trn@{\bf trn} command!article selection mode!commands} If you want to ke

rticle selection mode!commands} If you want to keep an article, you can save it as a file. To do so, enter the command {\bf s} or {\bf S} followed by the filename. If the file exists, the article is appended to the end of it. If the file does not ex

appended to the end of it. If the file does not exist, you are asked if you want to save the file in {\bf mailbox} format or {\bf normal} format. You should choose the normal format. \subsection{For More Information} \noindent If you want more inf

More Information} \noindent If you want more information about {\bf trn} enter the command: \begin{quote} {\tt \% }{\bf man trn} \end{quote} \section{Reading News with {\bf tin}} {\bf Tin} is a full-screen news reader that is also popular. To r

ll-screen news reader that is also popular. To run it, type: \begin{quote} {\tt \% }{\bf tin -r -q}\index{tin@{\bf tin} command} \end{quote} If you don't use the {\bf -q} flag you will be prompted for subscription to {\it many} newsgroups. You mus

for subscription to {\it many} newsgroups. You must use the {\bf -r} flag or you will get an error message: \begin{quote} {\tt Reading news active file...Can't open /active. Try tin -r to read news via NNTP.} \end{quote} You should see something lik

a NNTP.} \end{quote} You should see something like this: {\tt \begin{quote} tin 1.2 PL2 [UNIX] (c) Copyright 1991-93 Iain Lea. \\ Connecting to agate.berkeley.edu... \\ Reading news active file... \\ Reading attributes file... \\ Reading newsgroups

\ Reading attributes file... \\ Reading newsgroups file... \\ \end{quote} } Then the {\bf tin} menu should appear: {\tt \begin{quote} Group Selection (agate.berkeley.edu 36) h=help \\ \\ 1 1 ucb.class.cs61a cs61a class newsgroup \\ 2 ucb.cs.ms

lass.cs61a cs61a class newsgroup \\ 2 ucb.cs.msgs A gatewayed 'Msgs' list concerning CS. \\ 3 ucb.cs.undergrads Discussion group for Computer Science ugrads \\ \\ =set current to n, TAB=next unread, /=search pattern, c)atchup, \\ g)oto, j=lin

nread, /=search pattern, c)atchup, \\ g)oto, j=line down, k=line u, h)elp, m)ove, q)uit, r=toggle all/unread,\\ s)ubscribe, S)ub pattern, u)nsubscribe, U)nsub pattern, y)ank in/out\\ \end{quote} } The menu should be self-explanatory, but if you wan

e menu should be self-explanatory, but if you want more information about {\bf tin} enter the command: \begin{quote} {\tt \% }{\bf man tin} \end{quote} \section{Reading News with Netscape}\index{Netscape} You can read network newsgroups from within

scape} You can read network newsgroups from within the Netscape program.\footnote{At this time, {\bf netscape} is not available on the DEC systems but it is installed on the HPs.} To read news, start up the program by typing {\bf netscape}. Select t
up the program by typing {\bf netscape}. Select the {\bf Directory} menu item at the top of the display. From there, select {\bf Go to Newsgroups}.
